title: Serving JSON (Building an API)
subtitle: Make your server speak the language of the web

sections.content:
So far, your routes return plain text with `res.send()`. That works for simple messages, but **real APIs don't return plain text — they return JSON.**

Remember JSON from Level 5? It's the universal data format of the web. When your phone checks the weather, when a website loads your profile, when an app fetches new posts — all of that data travels as JSON.

Express makes this incredibly easy. Instead of `res.send()`, you use `res.json()` and pass it a JavaScript object. Express automatically converts it to JSON and sends it with the right headers so browsers and apps know how to read it.
keyPoints: res.json() returns JSON data — the standard for APIs
Express handles the conversion from JavaScript objects to JSON automatically
This is how every API you've ever used works behind the scenes

sections.instruction: Add a new JSON route to your `app.js` file. This route will return the current time and date as structured data. Add it alongside your other routes (before `app.listen()`).

troubleshooting.problem: I'm not sure where to add this
troubleshooting.solution: Add it right after your other app.get() routes and before the app.listen() at the bottom. The order of routes doesn't matter much, but keeping them organized helps.

sections.instruction:
Stop your server with **Ctrl+C** and restart it, then visit the new route in your browser.

You should see JSON data with the current time and date — not plain text, but structured data with curly braces and key-value pairs.
sections.expectedOutput: {"time":"2:30:45 PM","date":"3/2/2026"} (your actual time and date will be different)

troubleshooting.problem: I see plain text instead of JSON
troubleshooting.solution: Make sure you used res.json() and not res.send(). Also check that you restarted the server after making changes.

troubleshooting.problem: The JSON looks weird in my browser
troubleshooting.solution: Some browsers display raw JSON without formatting. Try installing a browser extension like 'JSON Formatter' for Chrome, or just verify that you see curly braces and key-value pairs — that means it's working.

sections.content:
Your server is now **speaking JSON** — the same language that every API on the internet uses.

Refresh the page a few times. Notice how the time changes with each request? That's because your server runs the code fresh every time someone visits the URL. It's *dynamic* — it generates a response on the fly based on the current moment.

This is the difference between a static website (same content every time) and an API (content that can change based on data, time, user, or anything else).

Remember Level 5, where you learned about APIs and JSON? You're now on the **other side** of that conversation. You're not just consuming APIs anymore — you're *building* them.
sections.tip: Try visiting /api/time from a different browser tab or even from your phone (if it's on the same WiFi). Each request gets a fresh response with the current time.

completionMessage: You just built an API endpoint that returns live JSON data. Every time someone visits /api/time, they get the current time. This is exactly how weather APIs, stock APIs, and social media feeds work.
